title: Light induced magnetization in d-wave superconductors
summary: We develop a microscopic theory of the inverse Faraday effect in d-wave superconductors. An extended version of the Keldysh-Nambu quasiclassical formalism is used to compute the dc-component of the current density induced by an external monochromatic radiation. Our work explicitly demonstrates how branch population imbalance produces nonvanishing nonlinear and nonlocal dc-response. We evaluate the magnitude of the induced current and obtain estimates for the induced static magnetization. Experimental implications of our theory and future extensions of our work are briefly discussed.

AI.tldr: Development of a microscopic theory for the inverse Faraday effect in d-wave superconductors using extended Keldysh-Nambu quasiclassical formalism to compute dc current density induced by monochromatic radiation.
AI.motivation: To understand and demonstrate how branch population imbalance produces nonvanishing nonlinear and nonlocal dc-response in d-wave superconductors, which was not previously explicitly shown.
AI.method: Extended Keldysh-Nambu quasiclassical formalism to compute the dc-component of current density induced by external monochromatic radiation.
AI.result: Explicit demonstration that branch population imbalance produces nonvanishing nonlinear and nonlocal dc-response; evaluation of induced current magnitude and estimates for induced static magnetization.
AI.conclusion: The theory provides experimental implications for observing the inverse Faraday effect in d-wave superconductors, with potential for future extensions of the work.

title: Ultrafast dynamics and light-induced superconductivity from first principles
summary: Experiments on superconducting materials have unveiled unique emergent properties when they are driven far from equilibrium. However, a quantitative first-principles treatment that describes experimental observations is lacking. In this work, we develop an ab-initio model for the nonequilibrium response of optically irradiated superconducting films within the framework of conventional electron-phonon-mediated superconductivity, leveraging new numerical techniques to solve the Migdal-Eliashberg equations directly on the real-frequency axis. This enables us to quantitatively reproduce the optical response of superconducting films in pump-probe experiments and validate our approach on measurements of the differential reflectance of Pb and LaH$_{10}$ in response to a pump excitation. Similar calculations performed on the alkali-doped fulleride K$_3$C$_{60}$ reveal that a photo-induced superconducting state is generated after irradiation by an ultrafast mid-infrared pulse of sufficient intensity, as reported in prior experimental work. The enhancement in this framework is attributed to the excitation of quasiparticles to energies resonant with the strongest electron-phonon coupling in K$_3$C$_{60}$, in close analogy to the mechanism for enhancement of superconductivity under microwave irradiation, explaining the nature of the photo-induced superconducting state and elucidating the subsequent quasiparticle and phonon dynamics. Our results suggest that photo-induced superconductivity is accessible in more materials than previously recognized. We demonstrate this by performing calculations on calcium-intercalated graphite, CaC$_6$, and predict a similar photo-induced superconducting gap.

AI.tldr: The paper develops an ab-initio framework using Migdal-Eliashberg equations to describe nonequilibrium superconductivity in optically irradiated materials, successfully reproducing pump-probe experimental data for Pb and LaH10, and predicting photo-induced superconductivity in K3C60 and CaC6.
AI.motivation: Experiments on superconducting materials show unique emergent properties when driven far from equilibrium, but a quantitative first-principles treatment describing these observations was lacking.
AI.method: The authors developed an ab-initio model for nonequilibrium response of optically irradiated superconducting films within conventional electron-phonon-mediated superconductivity framework, using new numerical techniques to solve Migdal-Eliashberg equations directly on the real-frequency axis.
AI.result: The approach quantitatively reproduced optical response of superconducting films in pump-probe experiments for Pb and LaH10. For K3C60, a photo-induced superconducting state was generated after irradiation by an ultrafast mid-infrared pulse of sufficient intensity. The enhancement is attributed to excitation of quasiparticles to energies resonant with strongest electron-phonon coupling. Similar photo-induced superconducting gap was predicted for CaC6.
AI.conclusion: Photo-induced superconductivity is accessible in more materials than previously recognized, with the mechanism explained by quasiparticle excitation to energies resonant with strong electron-phonon coupling, analogous to microwave irradiation enhancement.

title: Fast Real-Axis Eliashberg Calculations: Full-bandwidth solutions beyond the constant density of states approximation
summary: Experimentally relevant signatures of superconductivity require access to real-frequency quantities, such as the spectral functions, optical response, and transport properties, yet Migdal-Eliashberg calculations are commonly performed on the imaginary axis and then analytically continued, a step that is numerically delicate and can obscure physically relevant spectral features. Here we present a practical route to solving the finite-temperature Migdal-Eliashberg equations directly on the real-frequency axis, while retaining the effects from the full-bandwidth electronic structure. Our formulation accounts for particle-hole asymmetry through an energy-dependent electronic density of states, avoiding the constant density of states approximation often used in real-axis calculations, and includes a static screened Coulomb contribution. We introduce an efficient numerical technique to solve the Migdal-Eliashberg integrals whose computational cost scales linearly with the real-frequency grid, making high-resolution, full-bandwidth real-axis calculations feasible and providing direct access to the interacting Green's function and derived observables without analytic continuation. As an illustration, we apply the method to H$_{3}$S, where a van-Hove singularity near the Fermi level produces strong particle-hole asymmetry. The full-bandwidth solution yields noticeably different spectra than the constant density of states approximation and brings the superconducting gap and lineshapes into closer agreement with experiment, highlighting when band-structure details are essential. Furthermore, the methods presented here open the door to time-dependent, nonequilibrium simulations within Eliashberg theory.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a method to solve finite-temperature Migdal-Eliashberg equations directly on the real-frequency axis with full-bandwidth electronic structure, avoiding the numerically delicate analytic continuation step. Applied to H3S superconductor, the approach captures particle-hole asymmetry effects and yields results closer to experimental observations.
AI.motivation: Experimentally relevant signatures of superconductivity (spectral functions, optical response, transport properties) require real-frequency quantities. However, Migdal-Eliashberg calculations are typically performed on the imaginary axis followed by analytic continuation, which is numerically delicate and can obscure physically relevant spectral features. Additionally, constant density of states approximations used in real-axis calculations need to be avoided.
AI.method: The authors developed a practical route to solve finite-temperature Migdal-Eliashberg equations directly on the real-frequency axis while retaining full-bandwidth electronic structure effects. Key aspects include: (1) accounting for particle-hole asymmetry through energy-dependent electronic density of states, (2) including static screened Coulomb contribution, and (3) introducing an efficient numerical technique with computational cost scaling linearly with real-frequency grid size.
AI.result: The method was applied to H3S, where a van-Hove singularity near the Fermi level produces strong particle-hole asymmetry. The full-bandwidth solution yields noticeably different spectra compared to the constant density of states approximation, and brings the superconducting gap and lineshapes into closer agreement with experiment. This demonstrates when band-structure details are essential.
AI.conclusion: The full-bandwidth real-axis approach provides direct access to interacting Green's functions and derived observables without analytic continuation. The methods presented open the door to time-dependent, nonequilibrium simulations within Eliashberg theory.

title: Nb$_3$Sn Films Exhibiting Continuous Supercurrent Across a Diffusion Bonded Seam
comment: 11 pages, 8 figures, submitted to Superconductor Science and Technology
summary: Multiple pairs of bronze pieces were joined along a common seam and then exposed to Nb vapor via sputter deposition during heating at $\sim$715 $^\circ$C to form a diffusion bond between the pieces. Polishing and alignment of the pieces created smooth surfaces normal to the Nb flux with seams perpendicular to the surface (i.e. parallel to the Nb flux). Conversion of Nb to Nb$_3$Sn took place simultaneously with diffusion bonding, resulting in Nb$_3$Sn thin films that coated bronze surfaces and spanned seams with uniform thickness. Characterization of superconducting properties via magneto-optical imaging suggests that supercurrent flows freely across the seam in several examples when cooled to 9 K and shielding or trapping low magnetic field. Modification of the process to coat the pieces with Nb prior to diffusion bonding and Nb$_3$Sn formation resulted in varying degrees of seam coverage by the resultant Nb$_3$Sn films. The pre-coating method did not produce any example with quality comparable to the examples obtained by the hot bronze approach. This work could enable new approaches to joining Nb$_3$Sn materials in magnet conductor and RF cavity applications.

AI.tldr: This paper describes a method to form Nb3Sn thin films on bronze substrates while simultaneously creating diffusion bonds between bronze pieces. The process involves heating bronze pieces to ~715°C while exposing them to Nb vapor, resulting in Nb3Sn-coated surfaces that span the seams between joined pieces. Magneto-optical imaging shows supercurrent flows across these seams at 9 K.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to develop new approaches for joining Nb3Sn materials for magnet conductor and RF cavity applications, which require reliable diffusion bonds between superconducting components.
AI.method: The method involves joining multiple pairs of bronze pieces along a common seam, then exposing them to Nb vapor via sputter deposition during heating at ~715°C. This simultaneous Nb deposition and diffusion bonding converts Nb to Nb3Sn. The process creates smooth surfaces normal to the Nb flux with seams perpendicular to the surface. A modification was also tested where pieces were pre-coated with Nb before diffusion bonding.
AI.result: The result shows that Nb3Sn thin films coated bronze surfaces and spanned seams with uniform thickness. Magneto-optical imaging demonstrates supercurrent flows freely across seams in several examples when cooled to 9 K for shielding or trapping low magnetic field. The pre-coating method produced varying degrees of seam coverage but did not match the quality of the hot bronze approach.
AI.conclusion: This work successfully demonstrates a method to create diffusion-bonded Nb3Sn materials with uniform films spanning seams, enabling new approaches for joining Nb3Sn in magnet conductor and RF cavity applications. The simultaneous Nb3Sn formation during diffusion bonding produces better results than pre-coating approaches.

title: Spin-Charge Groups for Fermions in Fluids and Crystals: General Structures and Physical Consequences
summary: Known symmetry groups are not sufficient to handle various couplings between spin, charge and spatial degrees of freedom for fermions. To fill in this gap, we introduce the spin-charge groups (SCG) to describe the most general symmetries of fermionic systems. These groups are composed of the spin and charge operations as the `internal' symmetries, the spatial and temporal operations as `external' symmetries, couplings between them, and projective twists. After providing the general group structures of SCG, we study their potential applications in concrete physical systems, including $^3$He superfluids, charge-4e superconductors, collinear magnets with spin-fluxes, and superconductors with coexisting magnetic orders. We show that SCG can give rise to extra band degeneracies, Chern numbers and cross spin-charge responses like spin-supercurrents. Hence SCG provide a route to classify and explore new phases of matter even when strong interactions are included.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces spin-charge groups (SCG) as a generalized symmetry framework for fermionic systems, extending beyond known symmetry groups to capture couplings between spin, charge, and spatial degrees of freedom. The framework is applied to 3He superfluids, charge-4e superconductors, collinear magnets, and superconductors with magnetic orders, revealing new topological features and cross responses.
AI.motivation: Existing symmetry groups are insufficient to describe the various couplings between spin, charge, and spatial degrees of freedom in fermionic systems. The authors seek to fill this gap by developing a more comprehensive symmetry framework.
AI.method: The authors construct spin-charge groups (SCG) by combining: (1) spin and charge operations as internal symmetries, (2) spatial and temporal operations as external symmetries, (3) couplings between internal and external operations, and (4) projective twists. They then apply this general framework to concrete physical systems.
AI.result: SCG can generate extra band degeneracies, Chern numbers, and cross spin-charge responses such as spin-supercurrents. These effects emerge even in strongly interacting systems.
AI.conclusion: Spin-charge groups provide a new route to classify and explore novel phases of matter, offering a powerful framework that remains valid even when strong interactions are present.

title: Weak Localization and Magnetoconductance in Percolative Superconducting Aluminum Films
summary: In order to investigate the crossover from the homogeneous behavior to inhomogeneous (percolative) one, the temperature $T$ and magnetic field H dependence of the sheet resistance $R_\square$ have been measured for two-dimensional granular aluminum films. Fitting the theory to data of magnetoconductance near $T_C$ with use of the diffusion constant $D(T)$ as a fitting parameter, we have obtained the anomalous $T$-dependent diffusion constant $D$. From the analysis of $D(T)$, the electron diffusion index $θ$, a certain critical exponent in percolation theory, has been obtained. In the relation $R_\square-θ$, the value of $θ$ varies abruptly near $1.5kω$. This behavior suggesting the above mentioned crossover is similar to our previous results determined from the temperature dependence of the upper critical field. For percolative films in $H = 5\mathrm{T}$, we have found the strong $R_\square$ dependence of the prefactor $α_T$ in the expression$σ=[α_T e^2/(2π^2\hbar)]\ln T+σ_0$. The relation $α_T\propto1/R_\square$ can be explained qualitatively by a model of scaling law for percolation.

AI.tldr: This study investigates the crossover from homogeneous to inhomogeneous (percolative) behavior in 2D granular aluminum films by measuring temperature and magnetic field dependence of sheet resistance. The electron diffusion index θ was obtained through analysis of the T-dependent diffusion constant D(T), and was found to vary abruptly near 1.5kω, indicating the crossover. For percolative films, the prefactor α_T showed strong dependence on R□ following α_T ∝ 1/R□, explained by percolation scaling laws.
AI.motivation: The researchers aimed to investigate the crossover from homogeneous behavior to inhomogeneous (percolative) behavior in two-dimensional granular aluminum films. This crossover is a fundamental phenomenon in disordered superconducting systems that was not fully understood.
AI.method: The study measured temperature (T) and magnetic field (H) dependence of sheet resistance R□. They fitted magnetoconductance theory to data near Tc using the diffusion constant D(T) as a fitting parameter. From D(T) analysis, they extracted the electron diffusion index θ (a critical exponent in percolation theory). They also studied the prefactor α_T in the conductivity expression for percolative films in H=5T.
AI.result: The electron diffusion index θ varies abruptly near 1.5kω, suggesting the crossover from homogeneous to percolative behavior. This matches previous results from temperature dependence of the upper critical field. For percolative films in H=5T, a strong R□ dependence of the prefactor α_T was found, following the relation α_T ∝ 1/R□.
AI.conclusion: The observed behavior confirms the crossover phenomenon. The relation α_T ∝ 1/R□ can be qualitatively explained by a model of scaling law for percolation, providing a theoretical framework for understanding the transport properties in these granular systems.

title: Disentangling Shear and Compression Phonons: Route to Anomalous Magnetothermal Transport
summary: Magnetothermal transport in various frustrated magnets exhibits striking field-dependent anomalies that deviate from conventional magnon or phonon transport. Here we show that symmetry-constrained spin-lattice coupling naturally leads to mode-selective spin-phonon interactions that control heat transport. In the strong spin-orbit coupling limit, we derive an effective spin-phonon Hamiltonian in which phonons with different polarizations couple selectively to distinct spin operators. As a result, compression and shear phonon modes contribute to spin heat current across different magnetic-field regimes. Using a Landauer transport framework combined with exact diagonalization of spin chains coupled to a phonon bath, we show that this mechanism produces a characteristic peak-dip-peak structure in the field dependence of heat current, providing a microscopic explanation for field-induced transport anomalies in spin-orbit-coupled Mott insulators.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates magnetothermal transport anomalies in frustrated magnets with strong spin-orbit coupling, discovering that symmetry-constrained spin-lattice coupling leads to mode-selective spin-phonon interactions where compression and shear phonon modes couple to distinct spin operators. The theory predicts a characteristic peak-dip-peak structure in field-dependent heat current, explaining experimental observations in spin-orbit-coupled Mott insulators.
AI.motivation: Explain field-dependent anomalies in magnetothermal transport that deviate from conventional magnon or phonon transport theories in frustrated magnets.
AI.method: Derived effective spin-phonon Hamiltonian in strong spin-orbit coupling limit; used Landauer transport framework combined with exact diagonalization of spin chains coupled to phonon bath.
AI.result: Mode-selective spin-phonon interactions control heat transport, with compression and shear phonon modes contributing across different magnetic-field regimes, producing a characteristic peak-dip-peak structure in field-dependent heat current.
AI.conclusion: Provides microscopic explanation for field-induced transport anomalies in spin-orbit-coupled Mott insulators through symmetry-constrained spin-lattice coupling mechanism.

title: Polarization Dynamics in Ferroelectrics: Insights Enabled by Machine Learning Molecular Dynamics
summary: Ferroelectric materials with switchable spontaneous polarization underpin non-volatile memories, transistors, sensors, and emerging neuromorphic chips. Their performance and stability are governed by polarization dynamics and domain kinetics, making a microscopic understanding of these processes and precise atomic level control of polarization domains key challenges for next-generation ferroelectric electronics. Due to the limitations of the characterization technology with atomic level in experiment, high precision atomic simulations become important. First principles calculations are inherently limited in accessible length and time scales, making it difficult to capture the complex features of dynamic processes. Machine learning molecular dynamics (MLMD) offers a compelling solution by encoding quantum-mechanical accuracy into force fields, thereby enabling large scale dynamic simulations with near first-principles fidelity. This Perspective highlights the advantages of MLMD for simulating polarization switching, domain nucleation and migration, topological polar textures and curvature-driven ferroelectric phenomena, while providing a systematic overview of recent progress in these areas. We further discuss methodological challenges that limit predictive capability, including long range electrostatics, coupled lattice-spin degrees of freedom in multiferroics, and data efficient pre-training of large atomistic models. Corresponding advances in long range aware force fields, spin dependent machine learning models, and large scale pretraining are expected to move MLMD toward a genuinely predictive framework for the design of ferroelectric and multiferroic materials.

AI.tldr: This Perspective discusses how machine learning molecular dynamics (MLMD) can simulate ferroelectric polarization switching, domain dynamics, and topological polar textures with near first-principles accuracy, while addressing methodological challenges for predictive ferroelectric material design.
AI.motivation: Ferroelectric materials are crucial for non-volatile memories, transistors, sensors, and neuromorphic chips, but understanding polarization dynamics and achieving atomic-level control remains challenging due to experimental limitations and the scale constraints of first-principles calculations.
AI.method: Machine learning molecular dynamics (MLMD) encodes quantum-mechanical accuracy into force fields, enabling large-scale dynamic simulations with near first-principles fidelity to capture polarization switching, domain nucleation/migration, and topological polar textures.
AI.result: MLMD has shown promise for simulating polarization switching, domain kinetics, and curvature-driven ferroelectric phenomena, though challenges remain in handling long-range electrostatics, coupled lattice-spin degrees of freedom in multiferroics, and data-efficient pre-training of large atomistic models.
AI.conclusion: Advances in long-range aware force fields, spin-dependent ML models, and large-scale pretraining will move MLMD toward a genuinely predictive framework for designing ferroelectric and multiferroic materials.

title: Microwave Vortex Motion Characterization of Nb$_3$Sn Coatings for Applications in High Magnetic Fields
summary: In this work, microwave measurements carried out in dielectric-loaded resonators exposed to high magnetic fields are exploited to yield the surface impedance of Nb$_3$Sn superconducting coatings deposited via two different techniques: vapor tin diffusion, and DC magnetron sputtering. The obtained data lead to qualitative interpretations on both the Nb$_3$Sn superconducting properties, and vortex-dynamics and pinning, of each coating separately, as well as simple distinctive features when comparing those. When examining the respective surface impedances at varying field, it is expected that the studied films perform at substantially diverse magnitudes of flux-flow resistivity, but also in well-differentiated pinning regimes, yet the obtained surface resistances of both samples are comparable, thus demonstrating that there is room for film optimization at the expense of certain compromise between the parameters involved.

AI.tldr: This study uses microwave measurements in dielectric-loaded resonators under high magnetic fields to characterize Nb₃Sn superconducting coatings prepared by two different techniques (vapor tin diffusion and DC magnetron sputtering), comparing their surface impedance, flux-flow resistivity, and pinning behavior.
AI.motivation: To understand and compare the superconducting properties, vortex dynamics, and pinning mechanisms of Nb₃Sn coatings fabricated using different deposition techniques, and to identify optimization opportunities.
AI.method: Microwave measurements in dielectric-loaded resonators exposed to high magnetic fields were used to measure the surface impedance of Nb₃Sn films prepared by vapor tin diffusion and DC magnetron sputtering.
AI.result: The two coating techniques resulted in substantially different flux-flow resistivity magnitudes and well-differentiated pinning regimes, while the surface resistances remained comparable.
AI.conclusion: There is room for film optimization through compromise between the parameters involved, as the comparable surface resistances suggest that further improvement is possible by balancing the studied parameters.

title: Removing nodal and support-mismatch pathologies in Variational Monte Carlo via blurred sampling
summary: Variational Monte Carlo (VMC) is a powerful and fast-growing method for optimizing and evolving parameterized many-body wave functions, especially with modern neural-network quantum states. In practice, however, the stochastic estimators that form the backbone of the method can become unstable or biased due to the presence of nodes, a ubiquitous feature of quantum wave functions. In the continuum, this results in heavy-tailed estimators with potentially divergent variances, while in discrete Hilbert spaces the sampling distribution can miss parts of the support needed to form unbiased estimators. These statistical pathologies lead to unreliable optimization trajectories in stochastic reconfiguration or incorrect variational dynamics in time-dependent Variational Monte Carlo (t-VMC), and severely limit the power of the numerical simulations. We introduce blurred sampling to address these difficulties. The method has a number of rigorous properties that make it well-behaved, effective and efficient. Additionally it is a post-processing approach that can be used without modifying the underlying sampler and incurs only minimal overhead. We demonstrate its effectiveness on several representative examples where standard sampling approaches are known to fail, and apply it to large-scale problems in spin dynamics. This work establishes a broadly applicable framework for robust VMC and t-VMC calculations.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces "blurred sampling" as a solution to statistical pathologies in Variational Monte Carlo (VMC) methods caused by nodes in quantum wave functions, which lead to heavy-tailed estimators and sampling biases. The method is a post-processing approach with rigorous properties that can be applied without modifying the underlying sampler.
AI.motivation: VMC is a powerful method for optimizing many-body wave functions, but stochastic estimators become unstable or biased due to nodes in wave functions. This causes heavy-tailed estimators with potentially divergent variances (continuum) or missing support in discrete Hilbert spaces, leading to unreliable optimization in stochastic reconfiguration or incorrect dynamics in t-VMC.
AI.method: The authors propose "blurred sampling" - a post-processing approach that can be used without modifying the underlying sampler and incurs only minimal overhead. It has rigorous properties making it well-behaved, effective and efficient.
AI.result: The method was demonstrated on several representative examples where standard sampling approaches fail, and applied to large-scale problems in spin dynamics.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes a broadly applicable framework for robust VMC and t-VMC calculations, addressing critical statistical pathologies in quantum many-body simulations.

title: Long photoexcited carrier lifetime in a stable and earth-abundant zinc polyphosphide
summary: Halide perovskites have revolutionized optoelectronics by demonstrating that long carrier lifetime can be achieved in materials processed in relatively uncontrolled environments, whereas conventional inorganic semiconductors typically suffer from short carrier lifetime unless very carefully prepared and postprocessed. Here, we report the discovery of exceptionally long photoexcited carrier lifetime in monoclinic ZnP2, effectively bridging the carrier lifetime gap between direct-gap inorganic semiconductors and halide perovskites. Through computational screening, ZnP2 is identified as a long carrier lifetime semiconductor characterized by an unconventional polyphosphide bonding, combining covalently bonded phosphorus chains and polar-covalent Zn-P tetrahedra. Experimentally, ZnP2 crystals synthesized from low-purity precursors exhibit bright band-to-band photoluminescence at 1.49 eV and carrier lifetimes of nearly 1 $μ$s. Further analysis reveals that the polyphosphide bonding of ZnP2 suppresses the formation of deep intrinsic defects, making it defect resistant. Combined with its remarkable environmental stability, ZnP2 presents a highly promising material for solar absorbers and light emitters. Our work illustrates that underexplored inorganic materials spaces with unusual chemical bonding hold great promise for discovering novel optoelectronic materials.

AI.tldr: This paper reports the discovery of exceptionally long photoexcited carrier lifetime (~1 μs) in monoclinic ZnP2, bridging the carrier lifetime gap between direct-gap inorganic semiconductors and halide perovskites. Through computational screening and experimental validation, ZnP2 demonstrates bright photoluminescence at 1.49 eV and remarkable environmental stability, making it highly promising for solar absorbers and light emitters.
AI.motivation: The motivation was to address the carrier lifetime gap between conventional inorganic semiconductors (which require careful preparation for long lifetimes) and halide perovskites (which achieve long lifetimes even from uncontrolled processing). The researchers sought to discover inorganic materials with similar advantageous properties.
AI.method: The study combined computational screening to identify ZnP2 as a long carrier lifetime semiconductor, followed by experimental synthesis using low-purity precursors and characterization including photoluminescence measurements and carrier lifetime analysis.
AI.result: ZnP2 crystals synthesized from low-purity precursors exhibited bright band-to-band photoluminescence at 1.49 eV with carrier lifetimes of nearly 1 μs. The polyphosphide bonding (combining covalently bonded phosphorus chains and polar-covalent Zn-P tetrahedra) suppresses deep intrinsic defects, making it defect resistant.
AI.conclusion: ZnP2 presents a highly promising material for solar absorbers and light emitters due to its long carrier lifetime, defect resistance, and environmental stability. The work illustrates that underexplored inorganic materials with unusual chemical bonding hold great promise for discovering novel optoelectronic materials.

title: Polaron-driven switching of octupolar order in doped 5d$^2$ double perovskite
summary: We investigate how doping-induced small polarons impact the low-temperature multipolar orders of the $5d^2$ double perovskite Ba$_2$CaOsO$_6$. By computing intersite exchange interactions between 5d$^1$ localized hole polarons and 5d$^2$ magnetic ions from first principles, we demonstrate the reversal of the dominant octupolar exchange from ferromagnetic to antiferromagnetic. Solving the corresponding effective Hamiltonian we find this reversal to account for the progressive suppression of the ferro-octupolar order and the reduction of the ordering temperature upon Na doping. These findings clarify previously ambiguous experimental observations and demonstrate that charge doping in the form of small polarons offers a viable route to tuning intersite exchange interactions in spin-orbit-entangled materials, enabling the emergence of novel quantum orders.

AI.tldr: This study investigates how doping-induced small polarons affect multipolar orders in the 5d2 double perovskite Ba2CaOsO6, demonstrating that hole doping reverses the dominant octupolar exchange from ferromagnetic to antiferromagnetic.
AI.motivation: Understand how charge doping in the form of small polarons can tune intersite exchange interactions in spin-orbit-entangled materials to enable novel quantum orders, and clarify ambiguous experimental observations about ferro-octupolar order suppression upon Na doping.
AI.method: First-principles calculations to compute intersite exchange interactions between 5d1 localized hole polarons and 5d2 magnetic ions, followed by solving the corresponding effective Hamiltonian.
AI.result: The dominant octupolar exchange reverses from ferromagnetic to antiferromagnetic upon Na doping, leading to progressive suppression of ferro-octupolar order and reduction of ordering temperature.
AI.conclusion: Charge doping via small polarons provides a viable route to tuning intersite exchange interactions in spin-orbit-entangled materials, enabling emergence of novel quantum orders.

title: From Atomistic Models to Machine Learning: Predictive Design of Nanocarbons under Extreme Conditions
summary: The formation of technologically valuable nanocarbon structures under extreme conditions, such as those produced during high-explosive detonations, remains poorly understood but holds significant potential for the development of controlled synthesis pathways. While detonation shockwaves provide the HPHT environment required for nanodiamond formation, subsequent cooling and decompression dictate whether the diamond phase is preserved or transformed into other nanocarbon structures. Here, we employ GPU-accelerated ReaxFF simulations to investigate the graphitization and structural remodeling of detonation nanodiamond under nonlinear quench and pressure-release conditions. We further investigate how the initial nanodiamond morphology influences the resulting transformation products. Evolution of nanostructure, allotrope, carbon hybridization, and ring statistics are tracked. Rapid cooling combined with slow decompression optimizes cubic diamond retention, whereas slow cooling with rapid pressure release promotes surface-to-core graphitization, producing concentric sp2 layers and hollowed inner shells. Octahedral nanodiamonds evolve into carbon nano-onions, initially forming bucky diamonds that progressively transform into full sp2 structures, while hexagonal prisms preferentially form parallel-stacked graphite layers resembling carbon dots. Lonsdaleite emerges as an interfacial phase, suggesting potential reversibility in the shock-induced graphite-to-diamond transformation pathway transformation route. To extend predictive capabilities, we trained MLP regressors on over 10^5 node-hours of simulations. The model reliably predicts the number of graphitized layers from T-P trajectories with R^2 exceeding 0.90. Collectively, morphological control combined with optimized quench-decompression conditions promote the selective synthesis of nanocarbon allotropes.

AI.tldr: This study uses GPU-accelerated ReaxFF molecular dynamics to investigate how detonation nanodiamonds transform under different quench and pressure-release conditions, revealing that cooling/decompression rates and initial nanodiamond morphology (octahedral vs. hexagonal prism) determine whether cubic diamond, carbon nano-onions, graphite-like structures, or carbon dots are formed. An MLP model trained on simulation data predicts graphitization outcomes with R² > 0.90.
AI.motivation: Understanding nanocarbon formation under extreme conditions (detonation) is challenging but important for developing controlled synthesis pathways for valuable nanocarbon structures like nanodiamonds, nano-onions, and carbon dots.
AI.method: GPU-accelerated ReaxFF molecular dynamics simulations to model graphitization and structural remodeling of detonation nanodiamonds under various temperature-pressure trajectories; MLP regressors trained on simulation data for predictive modeling.
AI.result: Rapid cooling with slow decompression preserves cubic diamond; slow cooling with rapid decompression triggers surface-to-core graphitization forming sp2 layers; octahedral nanodiamonds transform into nano-onions via bucky diamond intermediates; hexagonal prisms form stacked graphite layers resembling carbon dots; Lonsdaleite appears as an interfacial phase.
AI.conclusion: Morphological control combined with optimized quench-decompression conditions enables selective synthesis of specific nanocarbon allotropes, with MLP models providing reliable prediction (R² > 0.90) of graphitization outcomes from T-P trajectories.

title: Tackling the Sign Problem in the Doped Hubbard Model with Normalizing Flows
summary: The Hubbard model at finite chemical potential is a cornerstone for understanding doped correlated systems, but simulations are severely limited by the sign problem. In the auxiliary-field formulation, the spin basis mitigates the sign problem, yet severe ergodicity issues have limited its use. We extend recent advances with normalizing flows at half-filling to finite chemical potential by introducing an annealing scheme enabling ergodic sampling. Compared to state-of-the-art hybrid Monte Carlo in the charge basis, our approach accurately reproduces exact diagonalization results while reducing statistical uncertainties by an order of magnitude, opening a new path for simulations of doped correlated systems.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the sign problem in quantum Monte Carlo simulations of the doped Hubbard model by combining normalizing flows with an annealing scheme, achieving order-of-magnitude improvement over existing methods.
AI.motivation: The Hubbard model at finite chemical potential is fundamental for understanding doped correlated systems, but simulations are severely limited by the notorious sign problem. While the spin basis in auxiliary-field formulations can mitigate this issue, severe ergodicity problems have restricted its practical applicability.
AI.method: The authors extend normalizing flow approaches (previously used only at half-filling) to finite chemical potential by introducing an annealing scheme that enables ergodic sampling. They then benchmark against both exact diagonalization and state-of-the-art hybrid Monte Carlo in the charge basis.
AI.result: The normalizing flow approach accurately reproduces exact diagonalization results while reducing statistical uncertainties by an order of magnitude compared to hybrid Monte Carlo.
AI.conclusion: This work opens a new path for simulations of doped correlated systems, making previously intractable parameter regimes accessible.

title: Asymmetric Energy Landscapes Control Diffusion in Glasses
summary: While diffusion in crystalline solids is quantitatively understood through defect-mediated atomic hops, no comparable quantitative framework exists for glasses. In these systems, the origin of large diffusion activation energies remains puzzling, despite local rearrangements involving low barriers. Using molecular dynamics simulations of metallic glasses, we decompose diffusion into random-walk and correlation contributions and find that back-and-forth correlated motion, not local rearrangement barriers, dominates the activation energy, resolving how low-barrier rearrangements yield large macroscopic activation energies. These correlations arise from asymmetry between forward and reverse barriers, a generic feature of disordered energy landscapes. We find that the correlation-driven mechanism is active beyond metallic glass alloys, including SiO2 and a single-component Lennard-Jones glass. The latter demonstrates that the correlation originates from structural disorder rather than chemical complexity. The framework also explains accelerated surface diffusion, where reduced activation energies arise primarily from weaker correlations rather than changes in local rearrangement barriers. Our results establish a direct, quantitative link between atomic-scale dynamics and macroscopic transport, providing a predictive basis for kinetics in disordered materials.

AI.tldr: This paper resolves how low-barrier atomic rearrangements in glasses produce large macroscopic diffusion activation energies. Using molecular dynamics simulations, the study finds that back-and-forth correlated motion, not local rearrangement barriers, dominates the activation energy in metallic glasses, SiO2, and Lennard-Jones glasses.
AI.motivation: While diffusion in crystalline solids is quantitatively understood through defect-mediated atomic hops, no comparable framework exists for glasses. The origin of large diffusion activation energies in glasses remained puzzling despite observations that local rearrangements involve low barriers.
AI.method: Molecular dynamics simulations of metallic glasses were conducted, decomposing diffusion into random-walk and correlation contributions. The study also examined SiO2 and a single-component Lennard-Jones glass to test generality.
AI.result: Back-and-forth correlated motion, arising from asymmetry between forward and reverse barriers in disordered energy landscapes, dominates the activation energy rather than local rearrangement barriers. This correlation-driven mechanism is universal across metallic glass alloys, SiO2, and Lennard-Jones glasses. The framework also explains accelerated surface diffusion where reduced activation energies arise from weaker correlations.
AI.conclusion: The results establish a direct, quantitative link between atomic-scale dynamics and macroscopic transport in disordered materials, providing a predictive basis for kinetics in glasses.

title: Optimization of all-optical phase-change waveguide devices for photonic computing from the atomic scale
summary: Photonic neuromorphic computing using chalcogenide phase-change materials (PCMs) is under active development. A key requirement is to enable as many optically programmable levels per cell as possible while maintaining relatively low optical loss. In this work, we report a combined theoretical and experimental study at the atomistic scale of a typical growth-driven PCM, Sb2Te, which reveals the unconventional optical properties of its metastable crystalline state for device design. Based on these findings, we come up with a "the shorter the better" strategy for Sb2Te-based all-optical waveguide devices, which yields a simultaneous improvement of both the programming window and the optical loss. In total, over 7-bit optical programming precision is achieved using a single waveguide cell, which is the record setting for all-optical phase-change memory devices. Our work is a typical example of the "from atom to device" scheme, which demonstrates the predictive power of in-depth atomistic understanding in guiding the design of phase-change photonic devices for improved performances.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a combined theoretical and experimental study of Sb2Te phase-change material for photonic neuromorphic computing, revealing unconventional optical properties in its metastable crystalline state. The researchers developed a "shorter the better" strategy achieving over 7-bit optical programming precision - a record for all-optical phase-change memory devices.
AI.motivation: The key challenge in photonic neuromorphic computing using phase-change materials (PCMs) is enabling as many optically programmable levels per cell as possible while maintaining relatively low optical loss. This work aims to address this fundamental trade-off through atomistic understanding of the material properties.
AI.method: The researchers conducted combined theoretical and experimental studies at the atomistic scale to investigate Sb2Te, a typical growth-driven PCM. Based on the findings, they developed a "the shorter the better" strategy for Sb2Te-based all-optical waveguide devices.
AI.result: The study achieved simultaneous improvement of both the programming window and optical loss. Over 7-bit optical programming precision was achieved using a single waveguide cell, setting a new record for all-optical phase-change memory devices.
AI.conclusion: This work demonstrates the "from atom to device" scheme, showing the predictive power of in-depth atomistic understanding in guiding the design of phase-change photonic devices for improved performances. It provides a successful example of how fundamental material science research at the atomic level can directly inform and optimize device engineering.

title: Continuous symmetry analysis and systematic identification of candidate order parameters for interacting fermion models
summary: Symmetry plays a central role in modern physics, from classifying quantum states to characterizing phases of matter through spontaneous symmetry breaking. In interacting fermionic systems with multiple internal degrees of freedom, however, determining the full continuous symmetry group and classifying possible order parameters remain challenging. In this work, we present a systematic framework for analyzing continuous symmetries and identifying candidate order parameters in such systems. By mapping the Hamiltonian to a Majorana representation, we obtain the generators of continuous symmetries from the Lie algebra of operators that commute with the Hamiltonian. We then identify the structure of this Lie algebra using the theory of semisimple Lie algebras. Building on representation theory, we further develop a systematic method for exhaustively enumerating candidate order parameters. By decomposing the exterior-power representations induced by the symmetry algebra on the Majorana space and incorporating discrete lattice symmetries, we classify these order parameters according to the symmetries they break. (Abridged. Please see the PDF manuscript for the complete abstract and specific model applications.)

AI.tldr: The paper presents a systematic framework for analyzing continuous symmetries and identifying candidate order parameters in interacting fermionic systems with multiple internal degrees of freedom. By mapping the Hamiltonian to a Majorana representation and using Lie algebra theory, the authors develop methods to enumerate and classify order parameters according to the symmetries they break.
AI.motivation: Symmetry is fundamental to modern physics for classifying quantum states and characterizing phases of matter through spontaneous symmetry breaking. However, determining the full continuous symmetry group and classifying possible order parameters in interacting fermionic systems with multiple internal degrees of freedom remains challenging.
AI.method: The authors map the Hamiltonian to a Majorana representation to obtain generators of continuous symmetries from the Lie algebra of operators commuting with the Hamiltonian. They identify the structure of this Lie algebra using semisimple Lie algebra theory. They then decompose exterior-power representations induced by the symmetry algebra on the Majorana space and incorporate discrete lattice symmetries to systematically enumerate candidate order parameters.
AI.result: A systematic framework for analyzing continuous symmetries and identifying candidate order parameters in interacting fermionic systems. The method enables exhaustive enumeration of candidate order parameters, classified according to the symmetries they break.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a complete approach to symmetry analysis in complex fermionic systems, enabling classification of order parameters by broken symmetries. The method can be applied to specific model systems to identify all possible order parameters consistent with the underlying symmetry group.

title: On the origin of non-Arrhenius behavior of grain growth
summary: Non-Arrhenius grain growth has been observed in a range of polycrystalline materials; however, its fundamental mechanisms, particularly whether the process is thermally activated or exhibits anti-thermally activation, remain controversial. In this study, SrTiO3 was employed as a model system to systematically investigate non-Arrhenius grain growth behavior through combined experimental and theoretical approaches, utilizing a newly developed grain growth model. The results reveal that non-Arrhenius grain growth is a thermally activated process without a definitive characteristic temperature, which is primarily controlled by the interplay between temperature-dependent factors and the temperature-independent parameters such as grain size and its distribution. Moreover, during abnormal grain growth (AGG), the non-Arrhenius behavior of grain growth primarily occurs at lower temperatures and gradually transitions to Arrhenius-type behavior as the temperature increases.

AI.tldr: This study investigates the mechanism of non-Arrhenius grain growth in SrTiO3 using combined experimental and theoretical approaches, revealing that it is a thermally activated process controlled by the interplay between temperature-dependent and temperature-independent parameters.
AI.motivation: Non-Arrhenius grain growth has been observed in various polycrystalline materials, but the fundamental mechanisms (thermally activated vs. anti-thermally activated) remain controversial and unclear.
AI.method: SrTiO3 was used as a model system. The investigation combined experimental approaches with theoretical analysis using a newly developed grain growth model to systematically study non-Arrhenius grain growth behavior.
AI.result: Non-Arrhenius grain growth is a thermally activated process without a definitive characteristic temperature, primarily controlled by the interplay between temperature-dependent factors and temperature-independent parameters (grain size and its distribution). During abnormal grain growth (AGG), non-Arrhenius behavior occurs at lower temperatures and transitions to Arrhenius-type behavior at higher temperatures.
AI.conclusion: The study resolves the controversy by demonstrating that non-Arrhenius grain growth is thermally activated, not anti-thermally activated, and provides new understanding of the temperature-dependent transition from abnormal to normal grain growth behavior.

title: Spatially Indirect Exciton Condensation in Two-Dimensional Strongly Correlated Semimetals
summary:
Identifying materials hosting an excitonic insulator ground state has been one of the major pursuits in condensed matter physics in recent years.
  Promising candidates in transition metal chalcogenide compounds (TMC), including $1T-\mathrm{TiSe_2}$, $\mathrm{Ta_2Pd_3Te_5}$, and $\mathrm{Ta_2NiSe_5}$, share a crucial common characteristic: their low-energy physics is governed by electrons in $d-$ orbitals subject to strong on-site Coulomb interactions.
  In this work, we investigate spatially indirect exciton condensation in two-dimensional semimetals on triangular lattice. Using a combination of dynamical mean-field theory and the determinant quantum Monte Carlo method, we study two- and three-orbital Hubbard models incorporating strong on-site ($U$) and inter-orbital interactions ($V$). Our results demonstrate that on-site Hubbard $U$ can strongly suppress the condensation temperature $T_c$, an effect that is particularly pronounced at higher electron-hole pair densities. This behavior contrasts sharply with the case without on-site $U$, where $T_c$ grows with pair density at fixed $V$.
  Moreover, we uncover competition among multiple electron-hole pairing channels in the three-orbital model, which also acts to suppress $T_c$ of exciton condensation. An orbital-selective electron-hole pairing state is identified. These findings may help explain the large discrepancy between strong binding-energy and relative low transition temperature for indirect excitons in TMCs materials, offering important insights for understanding and engineering exciton condensation in materials with strongly correlated $d-$ shell electrons.

AI.tldr: This study investigates spatially indirect exciton condensation in 2D semimetals on triangular lattice using dynamical mean-field theory and determinant quantum Monte Carlo methods. The research focuses on two- and three-orbital Hubbard models with strong on-site (U) and inter-orbital (V) interactions to understand excitonic insulator behavior in transition metal chalcogenide compounds.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand why promising candidates for excitonic insulator ground states in transition metal chalcogenide compounds (like 1T-TiSe2, Ta2Pd3Te5, and Ta2NiSe5) show large discrepancies between strong binding energy and relatively low transition temperatures. These materials share common characteristics of d-orbital electrons with strong on-site Coulomb interactions.
AI.method: The researchers used a combination of dynamical mean-field theory (DMFT) and determinant quantum Monte Carlo (DQMC) methods to study two- and three-orbital Hubbard models incorporating strong on-site (U) and inter-orbital (V) interactions on a triangular lattice.
AI.result: Key findings: (1) On-site Hubbard U strongly suppresses the condensation temperature Tc, with this effect being particularly pronounced at higher electron-hole pair densities. (2) This contrasts sharply with the case without on-site U, where Tc grows with pair density at fixed V. (3) Competition among multiple electron-hole pairing channels in the three-orbital model also suppresses Tc. (4) An orbital-selective electron-hole pairing state was identified.
AI.conclusion: These findings help explain the large discrepancy between strong binding energy and relatively low transition temperature for indirect excitons in transition metal chalcogenide materials. The study provides important insights for understanding and engineering exciton condensation in materials with strongly correlated d-shell electrons.

title: DeePAW: A universal machine learning model for orbital-free ab initio calculations
summary: Developing universal machine learning models for ab initio calculations is the frontier of materials cutting edge research in the new era of artificial intelligence. Here, we present the Deep Augment Way model (DeePAW) that is a universal machine learning (ML) model for orbital-free (OF) ab initio calculations, based on the density functional theory (DFT). DeePAW is currently the best OFDFT ML model according to the three criterions, 1) covering the largest number of elements, 2) having the widest application capability to diverse crystal structures, and 3) achieving the highest prediction accuracy without further fine-tuning. These scientific merits and innovations of DeePAW are stemmed from the novel SE(3)-equivariant double massage passing neuron networks. Besides predicting electron density distributions, DeePAW predicts formation energies of crystals as well and therefore paves an efficient avenue for multiscale materials modeling beyond conventional electronic structure calculation methods.

AI.tldr: DeePAW is a universal machine learning model for orbital-free ab initio calculations based on density functional theory (DFT), representing the best OFDFT ML model covering the most elements, widest crystal structure applications, and highest prediction accuracy without fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Developing universal machine learning models for ab initio calculations is the frontier of materials research in the AI era, aiming to overcome limitations of conventional electronic structure calculation methods.
AI.method: DeePAW employs novel SE(3)-equivariant double message passing neuron networks to predict both electron density distributions and formation energies of crystals.
AI.result: DeePAW achieves the best performance among OFDFT ML models according to three criteria: 1) covering the largest number of elements, 2) having the widest application capability to diverse crystal structures, and 3) achieving the highest prediction accuracy without further fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: DeePAW paves an efficient avenue for multiscale materials modeling beyond conventional electronic structure calculation methods.

title: Observation of Resonance of Kagome Flat Band Doublet
summary: The interplay between local and itinerant electrons underpins many correlated and topological quantum states. Kagome lattices provide an ideal platform by hosting both flat (localized states) and dispersive bands (itinerant states), yet direct spectroscopic evidence of their dynamical coupling has remained elusive. Here we report the long-sought flat band resonance in the quasi-two-dimensional kagome bilayer material CsCr6Sb6. Using angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy, transport measurements, and combined density functional theory and dynamical mean-field theory, we identify coexisting flat band doublets and dispersive bands near the Fermi energy. Upon cooling, the flat and dispersive bands exhibit a pronounced enhancement of spectral weight and hybridization, directly evidencing flat band resonance. Crucially, this emergence coincides with the onset of short-range antiferromagnetic correlations, contrasting sharply with conventional Kondo lattice behavior. Our findings demonstrate not only the long-sought flat band resonance in kagome materials, but also its unconventional correlation with magnetism.

AI.tldr: This paper reports the first direct observation of flat band resonance in the kagome bilayer material CsCr6Sb6, where coexisting flat and dispersive bands show enhanced spectral weight and hybridization upon cooling, linked to short-range antiferromagnetic correlations rather than conventional Kondo lattice behavior.
AI.motivation: The motivation was to find direct spectroscopic evidence of dynamical coupling between localized (flat band) and itinerant (dispersive band) electrons in kagome lattices, which host both types of bands but where such coupling has been previously elusive.
AI.method: The study combined angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES), transport measurements, and theoretical calculations using density functional theory combined with dynamical mean-field theory (DFT+DMFT).
AI.result: The researchers identified coexisting flat band doublets and dispersive bands near the Fermi energy in CsCr6Sb6. Upon cooling, they observed pronounced enhancement of spectral weight and hybridization between the flat and dispersive bands, providing direct evidence of flat band resonance. This emergence coincided with the onset of short-range antiferromagnetic correlations.
AI.conclusion: The findings demonstrate the long-sought flat band resonance in kagome materials and reveal its unconventional correlation with magnetism, differing from conventional Kondo lattice behavior.

title: Surface-related white light emission phenomenon in transparent solids
summary: Laser induced white emission (LIWE) caused by infrared laser excitation in Cr:YAG transparent ceramics was investigated. It was found that ceramics generates bright LIWE for excitation powers above a critical threshold. The LIWE was observed on the surface but not in the bulk on both sides of the sample. The vacuum conditions are required to observe LIWE. This phenomenon was discussed within the frame of Inter-Valence Charge Transfer (IVCT) mechanism in the Cr3+/Cr4+ ion pair.

title: Fermi surface of Kagome metal CsCr$_3$Sb$_5$ observed by laser photoemission microscopy
summary: We investigated the Fermi surface (FS) in the paramagnetic state of Kagome metal CsCr$_3$Sb$_5$ by employing a laser photoemission microscopy. We found a circular FS and two hexagonal FSs around the Brillouin zone (BZ) center. Polarization-dependent measurements further enable us to detect small FS pockets at the BZ boundary. According to the density functional theory calculations, the orbital characters of the FSs were determined from their shape and orientations. We found that the size of the FS is strongly modified for the d$_{xz}$ orbital, suggesting the orbital-dependent correlation effect. These results provide an electronic basis for exploring the interplay of antiferromagnetic/charge density wave order and possible unconventional superconductivity in this compound.

AI.tldr: Study of Fermi surface in Kagome metal CsCr3Sb5 using laser photoemission microscopy, revealing circular and hexagonal Fermi surfaces with orbital-dependent correlation effects.
AI.motivation: To investigate the electronic structure and Fermi surface geometry in the paramagnetic state of Kagome metal CsCr3Sb5, providing an electronic basis for understanding the interplay between antiferromagnetic/charge density wave order and superconductivity.
AI.method: Laser photoemission microscopy combined with polarization-dependent measurements and density functional theory (DFT) calculations.
AI.result: Found a circular FS and two hexagonal FSs around the Brillouin zone center, plus small FS pockets at the BZ boundary. The d_xz orbital shows strongly modified FS size, indicating orbital-dependent correlation effects.
AI.conclusion: These findings provide essential electronic structure insights for exploring the interplay of antiferromagnetic/charge density wave order and possible unconventional superconductivity in CsCr3Sb5.

title: The influence of nonradiative relaxation on laser induced white emission properties in Cr:YAG nanopowders
summary: Laser Induced White Emission (LIWE) is the subject of research worldwide. Since its discovery, the understanding of this phenomenon has progressed successfully enough to reach industrial applications. However, a lack of understanding of the nature of this phenomenon limits its potential. This article is devoted to the study of the influence of nonradiative relaxation processes on the properties of laser induced white emission in Cr:YAG nanopowders. The concentration series of Cr:YAG nanopowders was synthetized by Pechini method. The microstructure, optical and LIWE properties were studied. The influence of chromium concentration on the number of photons involved in LIWE process (N parameter) is shown. The increase of N parameter is associated with an increase in the probability of non-radiative recombination processes with an increase of chromium concentration. A multiphoton ionization model is proposed to describe LIWE phenomenon.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates Laser Induced White Emission (LIWE) in Cr:YAG nanopowders, focusing on how nonradiative relaxation processes affect LIWE properties. Cr:YAG nanopowders with varying chromium concentrations were synthesized using the Pechini method and studied for their microstructure, optical, and LIWE properties.
AI.motivation: Despite progress in understanding LIWE and its industrial applications, a lack of fundamental understanding of the phenomenon limits its potential. This study aims to fill this knowledge gap by investigating the influence of nonradiative relaxation processes on LIWE properties.
AI.method: Cr:YAG nanopowders with varying chromium concentrations were synthesized using the Pechini method. The microstructure, optical properties, and LIWE characteristics were studied. The influence of chromium concentration on the number of photons involved in the LIWE process (N parameter) was analyzed.
AI.result: The N parameter (number of photons involved in LIWE) increases with chromium concentration. This increase is associated with an elevated probability of non-radiative recombination processes at higher chromium concentrations.
AI.conclusion: A multiphoton ionization model is proposed to describe the LIWE phenomenon, suggesting that LIWE involves multiphoton processes rather than simple single-photon emission mechanisms.

title: Photoemission Signatures of Photoinduced Carriers and Excitons in One-Dimensional Mott Insulators
summary: We theoretically study photoemission spectra for photodoped one-dimensional Mott insulators that can host excitons, and show that their spectral characteristics differ qualitatively from those of photodoped semiconductors. In conventional semiconductors, photoemission spectra are well understood; free charge carriers generate spectral weight near the bottom of the conduction band, while the formation of excitons leads to replica features of the valence band appearing inside the band gap. In one-dimensional Mott insulators, on the other hand, strong correlations give rise to fractionalized elementary excitations-spinons, holons, and doublons-which fundamentally modify the photoemission response. We find that when photodoped carriers, i.e., doublons and holons, remain unbound, the photoemission spectrum directly reflects the dispersion of spinons, i.e., magnetic elementary excitations. In contrast, when a doublon and a holon form an excitonic bound state, replica structures of the lower Hubbard band emerge inside the Mott gap, carrying contributions from both spinon and holon excitations. Importantly, the distribution of the in-gap signal depends sensitively on the degree of doublon-holon binding. The origin of these spectral features is clarified through a combination of exact diagonalization and the slave-particle approach. These results indicate that photoemission from photoinduced carriers and excitons in strongly correlated electron systems can provide information on magnetic properties and carrier-binding properties.

AI.tldr: This paper theoretically investigates photoemission spectra in photodoped one-dimensional Mott insulators, showing distinct spectral features compared to semiconductors. When doublons and holons remain unbound, the spectrum reflects spinon dispersion. When they form bound excitons, replica structures of the lower Hubbard band appear inside the Mott gap, with the in-gap signal distribution depending on binding strength.
AI.motivation: Understanding photoemission spectra in strongly correlated electron systems, particularly 1D Mott insulators, where conventional semiconductor band theory fails due to strong electron correlations and fractionalized excitations (spinons, holons, doublons).
AI.method: Combined approach using exact diagonalization and slave-particle approach to study photoemission spectra in photodoped 1D Mott insulators.
AI.result: Two distinct spectral regimes: (1) Unbound doublons/holons produce spectra reflecting spinon dispersion; (2) Bound doublon-holon excitons create replica of lower Hubbard band inside Mott gap, with contributions from both spinon and holon excitations. The in-gap signal distribution depends sensitively on doublon-holon binding strength.
AI.conclusion: Photoemission from photoinduced carriers and excitons in strongly correlated systems can reveal magnetic properties and carrier-binding properties, providing a experimental probe for studying these systems.

title: Spectroscopic properties of Cr,Yb:YAG nanocrystals under intense NIR radiation
summary: Laser induced white emission (LIWE) was thoroughly studied in recent decades. However, despite the progress in understanding of this phenomenon, the mechanism behind LIWE remains unclear. The present paper focuses on the influence of Yb content on the LIWE properties of Cr,Yb:YAG nanocrystals. Microstructure and optical properties of the samples were characterized and the influence of the concentration of Yb3+ ions on the spectroscopic properties of Cr,Yb:YAG and energy transfer processes between Cr3+ and Yb3+ ions was revealed. Multiphoton ionization theory was used to explain the findings of the paper.
